description: Time reported here is only grossly approximate; it could be off by 1-3 hours (I didn't note the time in my field notes, for some damned reason!).  Jason noted swifts overhead while we were in the RMBO back yard setting up for an event that day and he immediately identified them as Black Swifts.  I looked up and agreed with his ID, raced inside the office to get my binocular, and watched them for <1 minute as they drifted out of sight.  The five birds were identical enough that I here describe them as one.  It was a large swift with very long wings and medium-length tail that sported a prominent notch when closed, but showing a square tail when spread.  The wings were fairly broad-based, being nearly centered on the body, with body projection behind wings being only somewhat longer than projection in front of wings.  The hands were fairly broad relative to that of other swifts, lacking the strongly tapered wingtip of the other regularly occurring ABA-area swifts. Wingbeats were almost languid for a swift, without the fluttery appearance of Chaetura.  Coloration appeared entirely dark, with no obvious paler areas, but distance would have precluded detecting anything as subtle as the white fringes of juvenile Black Swift, but would not have precluded detecting anything as obvious as the white patches on White-throated or White-collared swifts.
